title: Battle of Lepanto
summary: On the morning of 7 October 1571, the fleets of the Holy League and the Ottoman Empire met in the mouth of the Gulf of Patras off the western coast of Greece. In four hours of grinding melee, two hundred galleys and six massive Venetian galleasses under the twenty-four-year-old Don John of Austria destroyed the Ottoman fleet of Kapudan Ali Pasha, freed some fifteen thousand Christian galley slaves, and gave Catholic Europe its first great naval victory over Constantinople since 1453. Militarily reversible within a year, symbolically the battle marked the end of the myth of Ottoman invincibility at sea.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
For a century after 1453 Ottoman naval power had grown from a Balkan coastal fleet into a Mediterranean-wide instrument of empire. Under Suleiman the Magnificent the corsair Hayreddin Barbarossa, converted from Aegean pirate to Kapudan Pasha, took Algiers in 1516, threw back Charles V from Tunis in 1534, and defeated a Holy League fleet under Andrea Doria at Preveza in 1538. By the middle of the sixteenth century the Ottomans controlled North Africa from Algiers to Tripoli, dominated the Aegean, and threatened Sicily, Naples, and the Adriatic. The Venetian Republic, whose maritime empire in the eastern Mediterranean depended on precarious relations with Istanbul, lost outpost after outpost through the century.

The immediate war began over Cyprus. In the summer of 1570 Sultan Selim II, the son of Suleiman, invaded the island under the pretext that its Venetian rulers had allowed corsairs to prey on Ottoman shipping. Nicosia fell on 9 September 1570 after a forty-five-day siege, with a massacre that killed perhaps twenty thousand of its inhabitants. Famagusta, the last Venetian stronghold, held out for eleven months under Marcantonio Bragadin; when it finally surrendered on 1 August 1571 the promised safe conduct was violated and Bragadin was flayed alive in the marketplace, an atrocity that shocked Christian Europe and gave the Holy League its rallying image.

Pope Pius V, an ascetic Dominican elected in 1566, had spent his pontificate trying to construct an anti-Ottoman coalition. His envoys shuttled between Madrid, Venice, and Vienna, and on 25 May 1571 a Holy League was formally proclaimed at Rome between the Papal States, Spain under Philip II, Venice, Genoa, the Duchy of Savoy, the Knights of Malta, and other Italian states. The pope insisted that command be given to a young Habsburg prince acceptable to all parties, and the choice fell on Don John of Austria, the illegitimate half-brother of Philip II, twenty-four years old, who had already made a name for himself suppressing the Morisco revolt in the Alpujarras.

Don John assembled the League fleet through the summer of 1571. It sailed from Messina in early September: some 206 war galleys, 6 Venetian galleasses (large, high-sided vessels bristling with cannon), 24 large ships and smaller support craft, carrying about 28,000 soldiers and 40,000 sailors and oarsmen. The Ottoman fleet under Muezzinzade Ali Pasha, wintering in the Gulf of Patras with about 222 galleys and 56 galliots, was of comparable size but somewhat lighter in artillery and armour. The two fleets sighted each other at dawn on 7 October off the small port of Naupactos, known in Italian as Lepanto.

sections.heading: What happened

image.caption: The Battle of Lepanto, unidentified late 16th-century painter. National Maritime Museum, Greenwich.
image.credit: Unidentified late 16th-century painter, via Wikimedia Commons

sections.body:
The battle formed up in the classic galley line: three divisions abreast, with a reserve behind. Don John commanded the Christian centre from the great galley Real; on his right was the Genoese admiral Gianandrea Doria, on his left the Venetian Agostino Barbarigo, with the Marquis of Santa Cruz commanding the reserve. Facing them, Ali Pasha took the Ottoman centre from the Sultana, with the Egyptian corsair Mehmed Sirocco on his right and the Algerian Uluç Ali (the Italian-born renegade also known as Uluj Ali or Occhiali) on his left. A tricky north wind that morning shifted in favour of the Christians and briefly becalmed the Ottoman fleet.

The six Venetian galleasses, towed into position ahead of the Christian line, opened the battle. Their broadsides tore holes in the Ottoman formation before the galleys could close; contemporary accounts credit the galleasses with sinking or crippling perhaps twenty ships in the first minutes. The lines then closed at the standard galley speed, four or five knots, and locked together in a general grappling melee that stretched for miles across the mouth of the gulf. Musketry, arquebus fire, arrows, javelins, boarding pikes, and small cannon crashed through the crowded decks. Contemporary chroniclers wrote of the sea running red with blood and littered with turbans, corpses, and shattered oars.

On the northern flank Barbarigo, fighting near the shoals of Cape Scropha, was killed by an Ottoman arrow through the eye but his division held and broke Mehmed Sirocco's line, killing the corsair in the water as he tried to swim ashore. In the centre the Real and the Sultana grappled and were boarded three times; on the fourth attempt Spanish infantry led by Don John's guards captured the Sultana, and Ali Pasha was killed, his head hoisted on a pike above the Real. His two young sons were taken prisoner, treated well by Don John, and eventually ransomed. Only Uluç Ali, on the southern flank, kept his composure. He drew Doria out of position by a feint, cut off a squadron of Maltese galleys and captured the flagship of the Knights, but seeing that the centre and north had collapsed he broke off with about thirty galleys and escaped southward.

By about five in the afternoon the fighting was over. The Ottoman fleet was destroyed: perhaps 210 of its ships were sunk, burnt, or captured, and only some 30 escaped with Uluç Ali to Constantinople. Ottoman losses in men are estimated at 20,000 to 30,000 killed and about 3,500 captured. On the Christian side about 7,500 died and 13 galleys were lost. The most consequential single figure freed that day was among the 15,000 Christian galley slaves liberated from the Ottoman rowing benches; less consequentially, a wounded twenty-four-year-old Spanish soldier named Miguel de Cervantes, aboard the galley Marquesa, took two arquebus shots to the chest and a third that permanently crippled his left hand. Cervantes, who would remember Lepanto for the rest of his life as the highest occasion 'that past or present ages have seen or that future ages may hope to see', went on to write Don Quixote.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences

image.caption: The Battle of Lepanto (Allegory), Paolo Veronese, c. 1572. Gallerie dell'Accademia, Venice.

sections.body:
The news reached Venice on 19 October and Rome on 21 October. Pope Pius V, the story goes, interrupted a meeting to say that he had had a vision of the victory; he ordered thanksgiving processions and, later, decreed a feast day of Our Lady of Victory, later renamed Our Lady of the Rosary, to be kept every 7 October, on the theory that the League's success came through the rosaries said in Rome that morning. Cities across Catholic Europe erupted in celebration. Painters and poets scrambled to record the battle. Titian, Veronese, Tintoretto, and later El Greco all produced canvases; commemorative medals, tapestries, and sermons multiplied.

Strategically the victory achieved less than its symbolism suggested. Ottoman Grand Vizier Sokollu Mehmed Pasha reportedly told the Venetian envoy Marcantonio Barbaro that in taking Cyprus the Ottomans had cut off an arm from the Venetians, while at Lepanto the League had merely shaved the Ottoman beard, and beards grew back. He was largely right. Under Uluç Ali, promoted to Kapudan Pasha and given the honorific Kilic (Sword), the Ottomans rebuilt a fleet of some 250 galleys in the shipyards of the Golden Horn during the winter of 1571 to 1572. By the summer of 1572 the new fleet was cruising the Aegean again. In 1573 Venice, exhausted and financially spent, signed a separate peace ceding Cyprus formally to the Ottomans and paying an indemnity. The Holy League fell apart.

The deeper geopolitical impact was more subtle. Ottoman naval expansion into the western Mediterranean, which had seemed unstoppable in the 1560s, was checked; the sultans turned their strategic attention east toward Persia and north-east toward the Habsburg frontier in Hungary. The Habsburg-Ottoman rivalry that had shaped a century of Mediterranean warfare gradually cooled into an armed truce, and both empires increasingly outsourced their sea fighting to corsairs. The great galley battle of Lepanto, in which two hundred oared ships crashed into two hundred more in the classical Mediterranean manner, turned out to be the last of its kind. Within thirty years the sailing warships of northern Europe, the Dutch, English, and later French, would render the Mediterranean galley a museum piece.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
Lepanto entered Catholic memory as one of the great providential victories, cousin to the fall of Granada and the defeat of the Ottomans at Vienna in 1683. G. K. Chesterton's ballad Lepanto of 1911 revived it for English Catholic readers a generation before the Second World War, and Cervantes's Don Quixote made the wounded soldier's hand an emblem of Spain's early modern imagination. Every 7 October the traditional Latin liturgy still commemorates Our Lady of the Rosary, and the octagonal chapel of the Battle at the Doge's Palace preserves Venetian pride in the largest single share of ships and men the League fielded that day.

Modern historians have been more cautious. Fernand Braudel, whose Mediterranean of 1949 became the classic study of the sixteenth-century sea, argued that Lepanto was a strategic dead end that neither drove the Ottomans out of the eastern Mediterranean nor loosened their grip on the Balkans; the real Ottoman decline lay a century further on and had other causes. Recent Ottomanists such as Palmira Brummett and Emrah Safa Gurkan have emphasised the speed of Ottoman recovery, the strength of the North African corsair states, and the ways Lepanto has been overplayed by Catholic historiography.

What nevertheless remains undisputed is the sheer scale and horror of the day: perhaps four hundred large warships, more than a hundred thousand combatants, and the largest naval battle by ship-tonnage before Jutland in 1916. In the museums of Madrid, Venice, and Vienna, banners captured from Ali Pasha's galley and the standard of the Kapudan Pasha still hang; at Naupactos itself a small modern statue of Cervantes overlooks the bay where the fleets met. Whatever its long-term geopolitical weight, Lepanto marked one of those rare moments at which several worlds turned on the fortunes of an afternoon.

worldContext.text: In New Spain the second archbishop of Mexico, Alonso de Montúfar, had died in 1572; the Inquisition had been formally established in the viceroyalty in 1571 to police the doctrinal orthodoxy of the Spanish population. In the Andes Viceroy Francisco de Toledo was reorganising Peruvian silver mining at Potosí with the notorious mita labour draft. In Portuguese Brazil, sugar plantations were beginning to reshape the coast around Pernambuco and Bahia, drawing the first great waves of enslaved Africans across the South Atlantic.

Ming China under the Longqing Emperor had just concluded a peace with the Mongol Altan Khan in 1571 that reopened the trans-frontier horse markets and eased two centuries of intermittent war on the northern steppe. The same year the Spanish, sailing from Acapulco, founded Manila in the Philippines and opened the trans-Pacific galleon trade that would carry American silver to China for two and a half centuries. In Japan the daimyō Oda Nobunaga was tightening his grip on the Kinai region; Portuguese Jesuits, welcomed by some daimyō, had opened churches in Nagasaki and Kyoto.

The Mughal emperor Akbar, now twenty-nine and in his sixteenth year on the throne, had captured Chittor in 1568 and Ranthambore in 1569 and was consolidating his empire over most of north India. The founding of his new capital at Fatehpur Sikri began in 1571, the year of Lepanto. The Safavid Empire under Shah Tahmasp I was in its old age; his son Ismail II would succeed briefly in 1576. Portuguese Estado da India, based at Goa, continued to control the Malabar coast.

The Songhai Empire under Askia Daoud dominated the middle Niger; Timbuktu remained a great centre of Islamic learning under scholars such as Ahmad Baba's teachers. In Ethiopia the emperor Sarsa Dengel, only eight in 1571, was ruling under regents and coping with continuing Oromo migrations. In West-central Africa the Kongo kingdom under Alvaro I struggled with civil war and the rising volume of Portuguese slaving. In Mediterranean North Africa the Ottoman regencies of Algiers, Tunis, and Tripoli furnished the corsair fleets that Uluç Ali would command.

The Spanish and Portuguese continued to speculate about a great southern continent but had reached only fragments of Melanesia and Micronesia. Álvaro de Mendaña's 1568 expedition from Peru had discovered the Solomon Islands but not been able to relocate them. Māori communities in Aotearoa were expanding their classic pā fortifications; Polynesian societies from Tahiti to Rapa Nui continued their long tradition of oceanic voyaging. Australia's Aboriginal nations had not yet been visited by any European ship.

Northern Europe was preoccupied with a very different set of religious conflicts. In France the Wars of Religion had entered their third civil war; the Peace of Saint-Germain of August 1570 had just given Huguenots limited protections that would collapse in the Saint Bartholomew's Day Massacre of August 1572. In the Netherlands the Sea Beggars had captured Brill on 1 April 1572, launching the Dutch Revolt against Spanish rule. England under Elizabeth I was consolidating a Protestant settlement and beginning to sponsor privateers such as Francis Drake against Spanish shipping.

figures.name: Don John of Austria
figures.role: Illegitimate half-brother of Philip II and commander-in-chief of the Holy League fleet at Lepanto
figures.credit: attr. Juan Pantoja de la Cruz, Museo del Prado / Public domain

figures.name: Muezzinzade Ali Pasha
figures.role: Ottoman Kapudan Pasha killed aboard his flagship Sultana in the centre of the battle

figures.name: Uluç Ali
figures.role: Italian-born corsair, later Kapudan Pasha, who saved a squadron of Ottoman galleys from destruction

figures.name: Pope Pius V
figures.role: Dominican pope who assembled the Holy League and instituted the feast of Our Lady of the Rosary

figures.name: Sebastiano Venier
figures.role: Elderly Venetian provveditore who commanded the Venetian contingent, later Doge of Venice

images.caption: The Battle of Lepanto, oil painting by an unidentified late sixteenth-century artist, showing the interlocking Christian and Ottoman fleets and, in the foreground, the towed Venetian galleasses. National Maritime Museum, Greenwich.
images.credit: Unidentified late 16th-century painter, via Wikimedia Commons
